titleStmt.title: Letter from F. T. Smyth to the county inspector, Cork, 20 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: F. T. Smyth

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from F. T. Smyth to the RIC county inspector for Cork. The letter refers to an incident that took place near Charleville, county Cork, in May 1916. While passing through a field on his way home, Constable Prendergast was fired at. However, he survived unharmed with a bullet piercing a hole in his police cap.
Smyth states that Constable Prendergast is particularly unpopular with the local Irish Volunteers, due to his involvement in the arrests of leading Sinn Féiners in the district. Some of these detainees were mistreated while in custody, leaving the writer in little doubt that the shooting at the constable was carried out by a gunman with a ‘personal gripe’.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/5620/13399

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Firing at the person
Const. Ed. Prendergast 65841 County of Cork Cor Mallow 20 July 1916 In reply to attached wire I beg to
state that when Constable Edmond
Prendergast of Charleville was
returning to his station across
the fields at 5¼pm on 17th
May 1916 and when about half
a mile from his Barracks, he
sat down on a bank to rest.
After about ten minutes, a shot
from a revolver was fired at him.
Immediately after a second shot
went through the top of his forage
cap (not through the peak,)
which was on his head, and
pushed well back at the time.
The Constable them threw himself
over the bank to the other side, and
while doing so a third shot was C. F.
fired. The Constable, who had a
loaded revolver with him, went down
the field by the fence in the
direction whence the shots came from
but saw no person. On investigation
I found that the person who fired
the shots would have had time to
run along a bank - a fence - and
conceal himself from view
before the Constable reached his
point of observation. This Constable is obnoxious to
members of the Charleville Irish
Volunteers, in consequence of
recent activity in arresting Sinn
Feinners there, and more particularly
in January last when three
leading Sinn Feiners were
arrested on suspicion re malicious
injury to Mr Maddens Hotel. While
those prisoners were in custody at
the police Barracks, Constable
Prendergast, who was Barrack
Orderly, declined to permit them
to have any intoxicating drink. Between 7pm & 11pm Lieut.
Underwood on the 17th May 16
who was in charge of Military
detachment at Charleville,
accompanied by the police,
arrested on suspicion 14 Young
men — all Sinn Feiners — and
detained them in custody, until
the following day when Brigadier
General Stafford arriving at
Charleville Railway station, after
inquiring into the case, directed
Major Seymour Officer
Commanding troops at Mallow
to discharge all the prisoners. Except for the fact that they belonged
to the Sinn Fein organisation
there was no evidence against
them. After investigation and
inquiry I have no doubt but
that the shots were fired by
some Sinn Feiner and this
person may have had a personal
grudge against the Constable. As I have said, the
Constable while resting on the
bank had his cap pushed well
back on his head and the bullet
which passed through it could
do so without inflicting injury
to the Constable's head. The
allegation that he did it himself
is to my mind absurd. There was
no motive for it and there was
quite motive enough for some
Sinn Feiner to do it. Again
on the Constable's return to the
Barrack the revolver and
ammunition which he took
on duty with him were examined by Const. , who found
both correct, the revolver not having
been used. The Constable had
no other revolver. There was no doubt in my
mind, and I believe the military
were of the same opinion, but that
the Constable was deliberately
fired at. The Sinn Fein Section in
Charleville allege that the
Constable fired at his cap
himself but I doubt if they
really believe it. Police inquiries continue
but nothing has transpired. <closer F H Smyth County Inspector's Office,
Cork 21st July 1916
Submitted.
I made personal inquiry
into this matter at Charleville
on the 20th May, visited the
scene and interrogated
several persons, but I gleaned
nothing to throw any light
on it.
T.A. Howe
CI
